01

About Me

Aerospace Engineer

I'm Dinuka Hannadige, an aerospace engineering student at CU Boulder studying Aerospace Engineering Sciences with a minor in Engineering Management. I care about building things that work under real conditions, whether that's a competition aircraft, a heated ski wax tool, or a racing car aerodynamics package.

I'm a Propulsion Engineer on Colorado Design Build Fly and an Engineering Senator in the University of Colorado Student Government (CUSG) Legislative Council, where I represent the College of Engineering, work on legislation, and participate in the allocation of student fees across campus. I also work as an Engineering Peer Coach in the College of Engineering and have done work with BAE Systems in their Space and Mission Systems group.

Projects

P.01
Formula SAE
ER-08 Race Car
2023 - 2024
ERAU Motorsports
Competition Day: ER-08 on Track The ERAU Motorsports ER-08 (#106) navigating an autocross course at the FSAE competition. The aerodynamic nose cone and sidepods I helped design are clearly visible, generating downforce through the high-speed corners.
Chassis Delivery The freshly-delivered ER-08 tubular steel spaceframe (foreground) arrives in the ERAU Motorsports workshop, with the previous ER-07 visible behind it. The gold bow marks the moment the team takes ownership of the new chassis.
Bare Spaceframe: Front View A detailed frontal view of the welded tubular steel spaceframe. This critical structure serves as the mounting point for the double wishbone suspension geometry and the aerodynamic package I helped develop and validate.
Aerodynamics & Vehicle Dynamics Engineer

As a key engineering team member for ERAU Motorsports competing in the global FSAE collegiate design series, I contributed to designing, building, and racing a high-performance formula-style vehicle that balanced speed, agility, and cost-effectiveness.

  • Utilized Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) tools to design specific aero components, improving downforce and cornering capabilities of the ER-08.
  • Contributed to development of an aero rake and conducted pressure tapping tests to correlate CFD models against real-world on-track performance data.
  • Tuned the double wishbone suspension system to optimize mechanical grip and handling predictability.
  • Participated through Critical Design Review (CDR) and early shakedown testing, troubleshooting mechanical integration before competition.
P.02
Colorado Design
Build Fly (AIAA)
2025 - 2026
CU Boulder
Flight Test Day: Colorado DBF Team The full Colorado Design Build Fly team gathered at the airfield following a successful prototype flight test. Field validation sessions like this are essential for tuning our aerodynamic models and building pilot confidence before competition.
Prototype: Systems Layout Top-down view of our "Pink Foam" rapid prototype. This low-fidelity model verified the placement of dual propulsion motors, ESCs, servos, and the banner-release mechanism before committing to expensive composite materials.
Pre-Flight Integration Check Collaborating with the avionics and structures leads to verify control surface mixing, servo throws, and structural integrity of the airframe immediately before a test flight at the airfield.
Mission Verification: Aircraft in Flight The prototype successfully airborne during a validation lap. For the 2026 AIAA competition, the team needs to demonstrate sustained flight while towing a banner and carrying payload. This flight confirmed our propulsion system hits the required thrust-to-weight ratio.
Propulsion Engineer

On the Colorado Design Build Fly team competing in the international AIAA DBF competition. The 2025-2026 challenge was to build a Banner-Towing Bush Plane with short takeoff, banner deployment, and payload mission requirements.

  • Conducted static thrust testing on various motor and propeller combinations, verifying performance against thrust-to-weight ratio calculations for high-drag banner-tow missions.
  • Analyzed battery discharge curves to optimize energy density for multiple flight laps while minimizing airframe weight.
  • Collaborated with Structures to design and fabricate a custom Landing Gear Test Pad for empirical durability testing against hard landings.
  • Led propulsion avionics integration into the fuselage, ensuring proper cooling, CG placement, and reliable power distribution.
  • Hands-on rapid prototyping using foam cutting and composite techniques to build a lightweight and repairable airframe.
P.03
Colorado Design
Build Fly - v3
2025 - 2026
CU Boulder
Competition Day: Aircraft on the Flight LineThe completed v3 aircraft staged at the airfield ahead of competition flights. The twin-motor configuration reflects a clean redesign from v1, targeting improved thrust-to-weight ratio and STOL performance for the 2026 AIAA DBF banner-tow mission.
Wing Fabrication: Carbon Composite LayupComposite wing panel during build: black carbon fiber/fiberglass skin over a pink foam core, with a brushless motor already mounted at the tip. Patch repairs visible where the foam core was shaped and the skin was bonded.
Full Airframe: Workshop AssemblyWide view of the v3 airframe laid out across the workshop bench showing the full wingspan. The twin-motor wing tips and dark composite fuselage distinguish this design from the earlier foam prototype.
Avionics Integration: Wiring HarnessTwo team members routing the wiring harness through the fuselage, connecting ESCs, servos, and power distribution to the flight controller. The tight internal volume made component placement and cable management a real constraint.
Systems Check: Pre-Flight InspectionTeam member holding the assembled aircraft to assess balance and control surface alignment during a pre-flight systems check, confirming structural integrity before transport to the airfield.
Integration Review: Final AssemblyTeam inspecting the aircraft alongside the RC transmitter during final pre-flight checks, verifying control surface throws, motor direction, and power systems before heading to the flight line.
Bench Prep: Pre-Flight AdjustmentsPre-flight workbench with RC transmitter, wiring tools, fastener organizers, and the aircraft - the last round of adjustments before transport to the airfield for competition flights.
Nose Section: Motor & Propeller AssemblyClose-up of the nose motor mount and propeller assembly on the workbench. The brushless motor and spinner are fitted to the nose alongside the main landing gear - showing the structural integration of propulsion into the airframe.
Propulsion Engineer

Third-generation aircraft built by CU Boulder's AIAA Design/Build/Fly team for the 2025-2026 competition. The v3 is a full redesign from the ground up, moving from a foam prototype to a carbon composite airframe with a refined twin-motor propulsion system. The 2026 RFP required a Banner-Towing Bush Plane capable of STOL takeoff, banner deployment, and passenger payload missions.

  • Refined propulsion system selection for the v3 composite airframe, re-evaluating motor-propeller combinations against updated weight and drag estimates from the new wing geometry.
  • Supported avionics integration, routing power distribution and ESC wiring through the tighter internal volume of the composite fuselage.
  • Participated in pre-flight systems checks at the airfield, verifying motor direction, control surface throws, and CG balance before competition flights.
  • Contributed to composite wing fabrication, applying carbon fiber/fiberglass skins over foam core panels and mounting brushless motors at the wing tips.
P.04
KlisterFixer
Applicator
2026
CU Boulder GEEN 2400
Demo Day: GEEN 2400 ExpoThe full KlisterFixer team presenting at the Engineering Projects for the Community expo. Left to right: Molly More, Kristina Morgunova, Aiden Bunas, Cooper Smith, and Dinuka Hannadige.
Bench ValidationTesting the heating circuit with an Agilent multimeter and Keysight power supply, confirming the 11.3V / 1.13A / 12.8W operating point and sizing the LiPo battery for 1-hour field use.
Full PrototypeFinal assembled KlisterFixer in purple PLA showing the complete form: klister tube chamber, sliding cover, handle, and trigger arm. This prototype was used for field testing and the demo day presentation.
Design Iteration SketchesHand-drawn sketches with dimensions and a future fixes list covering trigger geometry, cover design, and LED slot placement. These fed directly into the next printed revision.
Field Test ResultEven klister coverage on the ski base after using KlisterFixer outdoors with the CU Nordic Ski Club. Application time cut from 15-30 minutes to under 10 minutes, with no direct hand contact.
Electronics & Systems Integration

Designed and built for CU Boulder's GEEN 2400 Engineering Projects for the Community course, in partnership with the CU Nordic Ski Club. Klister wax is essential for cross-country skiing in icy conditions, but applying it outdoors in the cold with ski gloves on is a mess. KlisterFixer is a handheld battery-powered tool that heats klister to 35-40°C, lets you squeeze it onto the ski with a trigger, and lights up when it hits the right temperature. No direct hand contact with the wax.

  • Designed and iterated the temperature-sensing indicator circuit using an Arduino Nano, transistor-switched resistive heating element, and dual LED feedback (green = ready, yellow = warming) to signal when optimal klister application temperature was reached.
  • Bench-validated heating element performance with a Keysight programmable power supply and Agilent multimeter, confirming 11.3V / 1.13A / 12.8W operating point and sizing a 1500 mAh LiPo battery for 1-hour field use.
  • Contributed to the iterative design process through sketches, design reviews, and hands-on prototype feedback, pushing improvements to trigger geometry, LED slot placement, and housing ergonomics across multiple printed revisions.
  • Supported prototype assembly, integrating LiPo battery, PCB, and wiring harness into the 3D-printed housing and foam-backed grip module across successive iterations.
  • Participated in outdoor field trials with the Nordic Ski Club, confirming the device works with ski gloves and cuts application time from 15-30 minutes down to under 10 minutes per person.
